text.title: Secrets Management Concepts
text.summary: Secret lifecycle, tool selection, dynamic vs static secrets, and JIT access patterns for 2025-2026.

rule.statement: Every secret must have an owner, a rotation schedule, and an audit log — secrets without these three properties are unmanaged liabilities.
rule.rationale: Unrotated secrets that are breached remain valid indefinitely. Without an audit log, breach investigation cannot determine when and which secrets were accessed.

list.item: Creation: generate with sufficient entropy (min 128-bit); never use predictable values
Storage: encrypted at rest in a secrets backend; never in code, .env files, or ConfigMaps
Access: RBAC with audit logging; each service gets its own identity, not shared credentials
Rotation: automated 30-day max cycle; applications must handle rotation without restart
Revocation: immediate revocation capability; test the revocation path before production

rule.statement: Prefer dynamic secrets (Vault database engine, cloud IAM short-lived tokens) over static secrets wherever the target system supports it.
rule.rationale: Dynamic secrets are issued per-request with a short TTL (minutes to hours). A compromised dynamic credential expires before an attacker can typically exploit it. Static secrets are valid indefinitely until manually rotated.

example.note: Vault database secrets engine: application requests PostgreSQL credentials, Vault creates a role with 15-minute TTL, credentials are valid only for that request lifecycle. On expiry, the role is automatically revoked.
Shared DATABASE_URL in .env file with a password that was last rotated 18 months ago and is used by 5 different services.

rule.statement: Use HashiCorp Vault for multi-cloud or hybrid environments; use the cloud provider's native secrets service (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault) for single-cloud workloads.
rule.rationale: Vault adds operational overhead (HA cluster, unseal management, upgrades). If all workloads are in AWS, the operational simplicity of AWS Secrets Manager outweighs Vault's cross-cloud capabilities.

list.item: HashiCorp Vault: multi-cloud, dynamic secrets, complex PKI, regulated industries
AWS Secrets Manager: AWS-native, managed rotation for RDS/Redshift, pay-per-secret
External Secrets Operator: Kubernetes bridge — syncs any backend into K8s Secrets
SOPS: GitOps workflows, encrypts files in repos, no runtime server required

rule.statement: Use OIDC-based authentication for CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions id-token, GitLab CI JWT) to eliminate long-lived service account keys entirely.
rule.rationale: Service account keys stored in CI secrets rotate infrequently and are accessible to all pipeline users. OIDC tokens are issued per-run, expire in 15 minutes, and are scoped to the specific repository and branch.

antipattern.description: Storing cloud provider access keys (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, GCP_SA_KEY) as CI/CD secrets.
antipattern.reason: Long-lived keys exposed in logs or to pipeline contributors grant persistent access. OIDC eliminates this risk entirely with no key to steal.
